## Stationary Classical Correspondence

The previous sections built the stationary gravity pipeline in M1 language:

$$
J_k^\pm
\longrightarrow
(\theta_0,\theta_i)
\longrightarrow
(G,A_i,B_{ij})
\longrightarrow
\text{local observer bookkeeping}.
$$

This section collects the classical comparison material in one place. Its purpose is not to replace the M1 variables with classical density, current, or metric notation. Its purpose is to show how the M1 stationary field package is calibrated against the familiar stationary limits of gravity.

That ordering matters. The previous sections did not start from classical density, current, or metric variables and then rename them. They first built a native map from positive momentum-source channels into scalar-well and directional field structure. The correspondence task is to check how that native map faces the familiar stationary language once the M1 construction is already on the table.

### Source-density calibration

The primitive source channels $J_k^\pm$ and their even packages $M_k$ have units

$$
[M_k]=\mathrm{kg\,s^{-1}m^{-2}}.
$$

Ordinary mass density has units

$$
[\rho]=\mathrm{kg\,m^{-3}}.
$$

The extra factor is one power of velocity. In the static isotropic scalar correspondence, the even M1 source packages are therefore calibrated by

$$
\sum_k M_k \longleftrightarrow c\rho.
$$

For an isotropic static source, this may be represented axis-wise as

$$
M_x=M_y=M_z=\frac{c\rho}{3}.
$$

Equivalently, if there is no directional imbalance,

$$
J_k^+=J_k^-=\frac{c\rho}{3},
\qquad
P_k=0.
$$

The six positive channels are therefore not six copies of the classical source. They are a directional-positive representation whose even axis packages sum to the scalar source $c\rho$ in the static isotropic overlap.

### Newtonian scalar limit

The M1 scalar field equation can be written as

$$
\Delta\theta_0
=
-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^3}\sum_k M_k.
$$

Using the static isotropic calibration gives

$$
\Delta\theta_0
=
-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^2}\rho.
$$

Define the ordinary Newtonian potential by

$$
U:=-c^2\theta_0.
$$

Then the scalar equation becomes

$$
\Delta U=4\pi G_N\rho.
$$

For an exterior spherical source,

$$
U(r)=-\frac{G_NM}{r},
$$

so

$$
\theta_0(r)=\frac{G_NM}{c^2r},
\qquad
G(r)=\sqrt{1-\frac{2G_NM}{c^2r}}.
$$

This is the scalar clock-depth correspondence. It is the check that the additive part of the directional source channels produces the usual Newtonian weak-field limit.

### Shift-current comparison

The directional side of the M1 field is built from the odd source package. In a stationary gauge-fixed comparison, the corresponding classical object is a transverse current-like source $j_i^\perp$.

The comparison equation is

$$
\Delta A_i
=
\frac{16\pi G_N}{c^3}j_i^\perp,
\qquad
\partial_iA_i=0.
$$

This equation should be read as a calibration and comparison statement. It does not replace $P_k$ as the M1 source of the directional field. It says how the stationary shift sector is recognized in the familiar current language used for weak stationary gravity.

The transverse condition is also a useful reminder. Not every raw directional source distribution is automatically a clean stationary shift input. In the stationary comparison problem, the directional source is read through its admissible or transverse part.

On the M1 side, the ingredients of this comparison are already fixed. The native odd-sector field equation is

$$
\Delta\theta_i=-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^3}\sum_kP_k\,e_{(k)i},
$$

the odd package is calibrated by $\sum_kP_k\,e_{(k)i}\leftrightarrow j_i$, with the same units bookkeeping as the even sector, and the shift potential is packaged as $A_i=-\kappa_A\,\theta_i^\perp$. Composing these gives

$$
\Delta A_i=\kappa_A\,\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^3}\,j_i^\perp.
$$

Matching this against the classical comparison equation above therefore fixes the packaging constant:

$$
\kappa_A=4.
$$

That single number deserves more than a silent substitution. The next subsection makes it explicit.

### Shift calibration and the factor 4

The scalar sector matched the classical limit with no leftover coefficient: the even calibration $\sum_kM_k\leftrightarrow c\rho$ fed straight into the Newtonian equation $\Delta U=4\pi G_N\rho$. The shift sector does not repeat that luxury. The M1 field map gives the scalar and directional fields with *equal* coefficients — both sectors ride the same $1/R$ kernel with the same $4\pi\kappa$ in the field equation — yet the classical gravitomagnetic equation demands $16\pi G_N/c^3$. A factor of $4$ separates the native odd-sector coefficient from the empirically anchored target, and that target is not negotiable: the Lense–Thirring readout behind it has been confirmed by the LAGEOS nodal-precession analyses and by Gravity Probe B.

This chapter refuses to absorb that factor silently. It is not moved into the source channels, whose grammar is fixed by the directional-positive construction, and it is not smeared into the current calibration, which is pinned by the same units bookkeeping as the even sector. It is placed in one explicitly labeled constant, $\kappa_A$, in the packaging step $A_i=-\kappa_A\,\theta_i^\perp$ — and its value is determined by audit rather than assumed. Appendix 3.6B runs the full stationary pipeline end to end, from a rotating source through $P_k$, $\theta_i$, and $A_i$ into the observer cross term and the precession readout, against the general-relativistic result. Two findings matter.

First, the observer layer is innocent. One could suspect that the transport bookkeeping $\Pi_i=p_i-p_{f,c}A_i$, entering the local magnitude quadratically, supplies a factor of $2$ through its cross term. The audit checks this directly: the linear response of the local energy to $A_i$ carries unit coefficient, exactly as the corresponding GR energy responds to its shift. No part of the factor hides in the observer readout.

Second, the whole factor therefore lands in the packaging, and its audited value is

$$
\kappa_A=4.
$$

Physically, this $4$ is not one mystery but two familiar factors of $2$. One factor of $2$ is the even-source sharing effect: in the classical description, the source that builds the scalar well also curves the spatial sector, and the shift readout of a moving body feels both. M1 currently matches the scalar sector through the clock factor $G$ alone and has not yet built the spatial-sector counterpart, so the shift calibration must carry that share on its behalf. The other factor of $2$ is the velocity cross-term structure of the test-body response — the same doubling that separates magnetic-type forces from naive scalar analogy in the classical gravitomagnetic equations.

This reading comes with a bookkeeping obligation. The spatial-sector construction — a $\gamma_{ij}$ deformation built from the even source channel — is named future work in this book. When it lands, it is expected to absorb one of the two factors of $2$ out of $\kappa_A$: the calibration constant of the shift packaging should then be re-audited and is expected to come out as $2$, with the other factor carried by the spatial sector where it physically belongs. Stating this now prevents the same factor from ever being counted twice.

Finally, the status of the number should be said plainly. $\kappa_A=4$ is a calibration, not a derivation. The framework has measured what its own conventions require in the frame-drag regime; it has not yet explained why. A derivation-track route exists — the null-channel source diagnostic of Appendix 3.3A already produces the factor $4$ natively under a further identification — and a boost-consistency derivation is planned. The lock criterion is fixed in advance: $\kappa_A$ graduates from calibration to derived structure only when those two independent routes force the audited value. Until then, the factor $4$ stands in the ledger as an honest debt, visible and priced.

### Metric-facing dictionary

For comparison with familiar stationary geometry, the weak-field dictionary may be written schematically as

$$
g_{00}=-(1+2U/c^2),
$$

and

$$
g_{0i}\sim\frac{A_i}{c}.
$$

The first relation reads the scalar package as the clock-depth side of the geometry. The second reads the shift potential as the mixed time-space, frame-drag side.

The schematic sign and coefficient in the second relation are intentional here. They depend on the stationary metric convention used for comparison. The robust point is the structural one: the M1 scalar package supplies the lapse-like side, while the M1 directional package supplies the shift-like side.

A stationary rotating target such as Kerr is valuable because it sharply exhibits the same split. A scalar clocking factor and an azimuthal shift appear as distinct pieces of one stationary gravitational structure. The full M1 rotating-source derivation remains a later burden, but the field grammar already shows where the two parts enter.

### Local clock and transport comparison

The local observer bookkeeping also has a classical stationary reading. In the M1 presentation, the scalar field dresses the fermic scale through

$$
p_{f,c}=p_fG,
$$

and the shift field modifies transport through

$$
\Pi_i=p_i-p_{f,c}A_i.
$$

In the corresponding stationary clock language, proper time combines a gravity-side contextual factor with a motion-side local factor. Schematically,

$$
d\tau=dt\,G\sqrt{1-V_{loc}^2},
$$

where the velocity is measured relative to the local stationary frame rather than relative to a distant asymptotic standard.

For Kerr-like stationary targets this takes the same structural form,

$$
d\tau=dt\,G_K\sqrt{1-V_K^2},
$$

with $V_K$ measured relative to the local dragged frame. The interpretive point is that gravity-side scalar dressing and motion-side slowing remain distinct mechanisms even when they appear together in one stationary clock law.

### What the correspondence establishes

The correspondence layer establishes that the M1 stationary pipeline has the right classical faces:

$$
\sum_k M_k \leftrightarrow c\rho,
\qquad
\theta_0 \leftrightarrow U,
\qquad
A_i \leftrightarrow \text{stationary shift},
\qquad
\kappa_A=4.
$$

It also keeps the status of those faces clear. Classical density, current, and metric notation are calibration and comparison tools. The main M1 route remains the directional-positive source-to-field map built in the previous sections.

The supporting calibration sheet for this section is collected in Appendix 3.6A.

What has been established here is stationary correspondence, not primitive ontology. What has not been established is a complete time-dependent gravity theory. The next section therefore changes scope carefully: it asks which pieces of the scalar-well and shift grammar survive when the field representation itself becomes nonstationary.
